name: Reverse Hyperextension
resistance_profile: Torso is fixed while the legs swing up behind the body; resistance peaks at the top where the erectors and glutes are fully contracted, with little spinal compression.
cues: Drive the heels up using the glutes and low back
Stop at a straight line — don't crank past neutral
Control the swing on the way down
execution_steps: Lie face down on the reverse-hyper pad or a high bench with the hips at the edge and the torso supported.
Grip the handles and let the legs hang down with a slight bend in the knees.
Squeeze the glutes and low back to raise the legs until the body forms a straight line.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower under control without letting the legs swing freely.
good_when: You want posterior-chain and low-back work with minimal axial loading
You want to add erector and glute volume while sparing the spine from compression
bad_when: You rely on momentum to swing the legs rather than muscular control
You aggressively hyperextend at the top with existing lumbar issues
common_errors: Overextending the lumbar spine at the top
progressions: Add load via machine plates or an ankle band
Add a pause at the top
